text.title: DX Friction Log Format Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules grounding the DX Friction Log Format methodology. Each pairs a binary check with a sourced rationale; the last rule binds the decision tree skip path.

rule.statement: Each log row captures exactly one friction moment with timestamp, surface, severity, one-line description, and proposed-fix. Multi-friction rows are rejected.
rule.rationale: multi-friction rows cannot be closed individually.

rule.statement: Severity is one of {minor, papercut, blocker}. Free-form severity is rejected.
rule.rationale: Comparability across weeks needs a fixed scale.

rule.statement: Surface column names the touched tool / file / command (e.g. `vitest config`, `pnpm install`, `vscode python ext`). Vague surfaces ('the project') are rejected.
rule.rationale: surface tells you which tool's PR queue to target.

rule.statement: Every row has a one-line proposed fix even if it is 'investigate later'. Empty fix column is rejected.
rule.rationale: Forces minimal thinking at capture time, which is when context is richest.

rule.statement: Triage updates each row with status ∈ {open, in-flight, fixed, wont-fix, escalated} and a triage timestamp. Stale open rows are demoted to 'wont-fix' after 4 weeks unless re-stated.

When the decision tree routes to `skip-this-methodology`, the agent MUST emit a structured `skip` decision (with reason + suggested-sibling) rather than running DX Friction Log Format under degraded conditions.
rule.rationale: decision-tree contract. The decision tree is the only place that knows the routing; skipping must be observable so the orchestrator can log the routed-elsewhere path.
